title: NIS2 — Scope and Critical Sectors (Arts. 2–3, Annexes I & II)
content:
Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (NIS2) replaces NIS1 from 2016 and significantly expands the scope of EU cybersecurity regulation. Member States had to transpose NIS2 into national law by 17 October 2024 and apply national measures from 18 October 2024. Transposition has been uneven — as of mid-2025, several member states (notably Germany, Belgium, Ireland) had delayed full transposition, but the underlying obligations apply via direct effect for the clearest provisions.

Art. 2 — Scope: NIS2 applies to public and private entities operating in the sectors listed in Annex I (sectors of high criticality) and Annex II (other critical sectors) that meet the size-cap thresholds: medium-sized enterprises (50+ employees AND/OR €10M+ annual turnover/balance sheet) and large enterprises. Smaller entities can also be in scope if specifically designated by national competent authorities based on the criticality of their service.

Art. 3 — Essential and important entities: NIS2 distinguishes two tiers with different supervisory regimes:
- ESSENTIAL entities: large enterprises in Annex I sectors, plus certain qualified providers regardless of size (DNS service providers, TLD name registries, providers of public electronic communications networks/services, trust service providers, top-level domain name registries, providers of public electronic communications networks, etc.). Subject to PROACTIVE ex-ante supervision.
- IMPORTANT entities: medium-sized enterprises in Annex I sectors, plus all in-scope entities in Annex II sectors. Subject to ex-post supervision (only after an incident or evidence of non-compliance).

Annex I sectors of high criticality:
- Energy (electricity, district heating, oil, gas, hydrogen)
- Transport (air, rail, water, road)
- Banking and financial market infrastructures (subset overlaps with DORA — DORA prevails as lex specialis)
- Health (healthcare providers, EU reference laboratories, R&D of medicinal products, manufacturers of basic pharmaceutical products and medicinal preparations, manufacturers of critical medical devices)
- Drinking water and wastewater
- Digital infrastructure (IXPs, DNS service providers, TLD name registries, cloud computing service providers, data centre service providers, content delivery networks, trust service providers, providers of public electronic communications networks and services)
- ICT service management (B2B managed service providers, managed security service providers)
- Public administration (central government, regional level under conditions)
- Space

Annex II other critical sectors:
- Postal and courier services
- Waste management
- Manufacture, production and distribution of chemicals
- Production, processing and distribution of food
- Manufacturing (medical devices, computers/electronics/optical products, electrical equipment, machinery and equipment, motor vehicles, trailers and semi-trailers, other transport equipment)
- Digital providers (online marketplaces, online search engines, social networking services platforms)
- Research

DORA / NIS2 interaction: financial entities in scope of DORA are excluded from NIS2 for the matters DORA covers (lex specialis principle, recital 28 NIS2). In practice this means EU banks/insurers/investment firms apply DORA for ICT risk management and incident reporting, NOT NIS2.

Use this article range when scoping which EU regulation applies to a given entity, or when discussing why a 60-person fintech qualifies under DORA but a 60-person healthcare provider qualifies under NIS2.

title: NIS2 — Cybersecurity Risk Management Measures (Art. 21)
content:
Article 21 of NIS2 establishes the minimum cybersecurity risk management measures that essential and important entities must implement. Unlike NIS1's high-level reference to 'appropriate and proportionate' measures, NIS2 enumerates 10 specific measure categories that competent authorities will assess against.

Art. 21(1): essential and important entities must take appropriate and proportionate technical, operational, and organisational measures to manage the risks posed to the security of network and information systems which those entities use for their operations or for the provision of their services, and to prevent or minimise the impact of incidents on recipients of their services and on other services.

Art. 21(2): the measures shall be based on an all-hazards approach that aims to protect network and information systems and the physical environment of those systems from incidents, and shall include AT LEAST the following:

(a) policies on risk analysis and information system security
(b) incident handling
(c) business continuity, such as backup management and disaster recovery, and crisis management
(d) supply chain security, including security-related aspects concerning the relationships between each entity and its direct suppliers or service providers
(e) security in network and information systems acquisition, development and maintenance, including vulnerability handling and disclosure
(f) policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk-management measures
(g) basic cyber hygiene practices and cybersecurity training
(h) policies and procedures regarding the use of cryptography and, where appropriate, encryption
(i) human resources security, access control policies and asset management
(j) the use of multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication solutions, secured voice, video and text communications and secured emergency communication systems within the entity, where appropriate

The Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2024/2690 of 17 October 2024 specifies technical and methodological requirements for these measures, applicable to certain digital infrastructure and ICT service management entities. National competent authorities are expected to issue equivalent guidance for other sectors.

Art. 21(3): when considering which measures are appropriate, entities shall take into account the state of the art and, where applicable, relevant European and international standards. ENISA-issued guidance and certification schemes (Cybersecurity Act, Art. 24 NIS2) are the de-facto baseline.

Art. 21(4): when an entity finds that it does not comply with the measures, it shall, without undue delay, take all necessary, appropriate and proportionate corrective measures.

Art. 21(5): the Commission may, by means of implementing acts, lay down further technical and methodological requirements. The first set landed October 2024 and addresses cloud, data centre, CDN, managed service providers, online marketplaces, online search engines, social networking platforms, and trust service providers.

Key practical implications:
- Art. 21(2)(d) supply-chain security is now a top-level legal requirement — not a 'best practice'. National competent authorities are explicitly looking at supplier governance during their first wave of inspections.
- Art. 21(2)(j) multi-factor authentication is the most operationally-prescribed control in any EU cyber regulation. The phrase 'where appropriate' has been narrowly interpreted — MFA is effectively mandatory for privileged accounts and remote access.
- Art. 21(2)(h) cryptography requirements are aligned with ENISA's quantum-readiness guidance (post-quantum cryptography migration on national authorities' radar from 2026).

Use this article when discussing NIS2-compliant control frameworks, when mapping NIS2 requirements to NIST CSF v2 or ISO 27001 controls, or when scoping the gap between existing security programmes and the Art. 21 baseline.

title: NIS2 — Incident Reporting Obligations (Art. 23)
content:
Article 23 of NIS2 establishes the three-tier incident notification cascade that every essential and important entity must execute when a significant incident occurs. The timelines are tight and have created operational pressure for CSIRTs across the EU.

Art. 23(1): essential and important entities shall notify, without undue delay, the CSIRT or, where applicable, the competent authority of any significant incident. The notification shall be made via a single entry point. Entities shall also notify, without undue delay, the recipients of their services that are affected by the significant incident that may adversely affect the provision of those services.

Art. 23(3) — Significant incident definition: an incident is significant if it has caused or is capable of causing severe operational disruption of the services or financial loss for the entity concerned, OR has affected or is capable of affecting other natural or legal persons by causing considerable material or non-material damage. National competent authorities have issued sector-specific guidance on what 'severe' and 'considerable' mean.

Art. 23(4) — Three-tier reporting cascade:
- EARLY WARNING: within 24 hours of becoming aware of the significant incident, indicating whether it is suspected to be caused by unlawful or malicious acts or could have a cross-border impact
- INCIDENT NOTIFICATION: within 72 hours of becoming aware of the significant incident, updating the early warning with an initial assessment, including severity and impact, and where available, the indicators of compromise
- FINAL REPORT: not later than one month after the submission of the incident notification, including a detailed description of the incident, its severity and impact, the type of threat or root cause that likely triggered the incident, applied and ongoing mitigation measures, and where applicable, the cross-border impact of the incident

Progress reports may be requested by the CSIRT/competent authority at any time, and entities must provide an intermediate report on relevant status updates.

Art. 23(5): where applicable, intermediate reports — interim updates on the status of the incident handling.

Art. 23(11): the CSIRT or competent authority shall provide, without undue delay and where possible within 24 hours of receiving the early warning, a response to the notifying entity, including initial feedback on the incident and, upon the entity's request, guidance on the implementation of possible mitigation measures.

DORA / NIS2 interaction: financial entities report incidents under DORA Art. 19 (4-hour initial, 72-hour intermediate, 1-month final) instead of NIS2. The two regimes are NOT compatible: DORA is stricter on initial timing, NIS2 is stricter on cross-border coordination obligations.

Key practical implications:
- The 24-hour early warning is shorter than NIS1's 72 hours and forces entities to have a pre-staged decision tree: 'is this incident a NIS2-significant incident?' must be answerable within hours, not days
- 'Cross-border impact' triggers additional coordination via the CSIRTs Network — entities operating in multiple member states must notify in EACH affected jurisdiction
- The final report's 'type of threat or root cause' field has driven adoption of structured incident taxonomies (MITRE ATT&CK ID references, ENISA threat landscape categories)

Art. 23(8): voluntary notifications of incidents, cyber threats and near misses are also accepted from any entity (not only in-scope ones). This creates an information-sharing pipeline that NCAs use to build sector threat intelligence.

Use this article when discussing incident response playbook timelines, regulatory reporting tooling, the build-vs-buy decision for incident classification automation, or when comparing NIS2 obligations to DORA, SEC 4-day disclosure, HIPAA 60-day breach notification, or other concurrent reporting regimes.

title: NIS2 — Supply Chain Security (Art. 21(2)(d), Art. 22)
content:
Supply chain security is one of the most prominent additions in NIS2 compared to NIS1, reflecting the post-SolarWinds, post-Log4j, post-Kaseya policy consensus that third-party risk had been under-addressed.

Art. 21(2)(d) names supply chain security as a mandatory cybersecurity risk management measure. Specifically: 'security-related aspects concerning the relationships between each entity and its direct suppliers or service providers, including assessment of the cybersecurity risk-management practices of the entity's direct suppliers and service providers.'

Art. 22 — EU-coordinated security risk assessments of critical supply chains: the Cooperation Group (the formal NIS2 governance body bringing together Member States, the Commission, and ENISA) may carry out, in cooperation with the Commission and ENISA, coordinated security risk assessments of specific critical ICT services, ICT systems, or ICT products supply chains, taking into account technical and, where relevant, non-technical risk factors.

The Cooperation Group has executed several Art. 22 assessments already:
- 5G supply chain (initial assessment 2019 under NIS1, updated 2024 under NIS2) — drove member-state restrictions on 'high-risk vendors' (de facto Huawei, ZTE) in critical 5G network infrastructure
- Cloud services (assessment ongoing as of 2025) — examining concentration risk and sovereignty implications
- Cybersecurity of network and information system supply chains used in the energy sector

Art. 22(4): the outcome of a coordinated security risk assessment may include recommendations to be considered by Member States and entities when making decisions on cybersecurity matters, including risk mitigation measures and recommended actions to be taken.

DORA contrast: DORA Art. 28–30 covers third-party risk for financial entities with prescriptive contract clauses and the register-of-information requirement. NIS2 Art. 22 is more strategic — it operates at the SECTOR level, not the entity level, and produces sector-wide risk advisories rather than individual contractual obligations.

Key practical implications for CISO supply-chain programmes:
- Entities must assess direct suppliers' cybersecurity risk-management practices. ENISA has published guidance on the depth of expected assessment (proportionate to the criticality of the service and the risk profile of the supplier)
- Supplier security questionnaires (SIG, CAIQ, custom RFI) are now the operational evidence — must be retained for supervisory inspection
- Entities cannot pass the buck: 'we relied on our supplier's SOC 2 report' is insufficient — the entity must demonstrate its own assessment process
- For sectors where Art. 22 EU-coordinated risk assessments are active (5G, cloud), entities must explicitly consider the assessment outcomes when contracting

The Commission's October 2024 Implementing Regulation (2024/2690) — which applies to digital infrastructure and ICT service management entities — specifies detailed supply-chain assessment requirements including: vendor selection criteria, contractual SLA requirements, monitoring obligations during the contract, and exit-strategy planning. The technical detail is comparable to DORA Art. 28-30 obligations for digital-infrastructure NIS2 entities.

Use this article when discussing third-party security questionnaires, sector-level supply-chain risk (cloud concentration, 5G vendor restrictions), the difference between NIS2's strategic third-party regime and DORA's contractual one, or how an EU healthcare provider should assess SaaS vendors under NIS2 Art. 21(2)(d).

title: NIS2 — Management Accountability and Sanctions (Arts. 20, 32–34)
content:
NIS2 introduces personal liability for management body members and a sanctions framework comparable to GDPR. This is the most significant accountability shift from NIS1, which had no equivalent personal-liability hook.

Art. 20 — Governance: management bodies of essential and important entities must:
(a) APPROVE the cybersecurity risk-management measures taken by those entities in order to comply with Article 21
(b) OVERSEE its implementation
(c) BE HELD LIABLE for infringements by the entities of Article 21

Member States shall ensure that members of the management body of essential and important entities are required to follow training, and shall encourage them to offer similar training to their employees, on a regular basis, in order that they gain sufficient knowledge and skills to enable them to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices and their impact on the services provided by the entity.

The 'BE HELD LIABLE' language in Art. 20(1)(c) is unprecedented in EU cybersecurity law. Member States have transposed this via personal liability for management body members (e.g. Germany's NIS2UmsuCG, Italy's Decreto Legislativo 138/2024, France's transposition law). Liability typically includes the possibility of:
- Temporary suspension of management body members from their duties (extreme cases, essential entities only)
- Personal pecuniary penalties (varies by member state)
- Disqualification from holding management roles in scope entities for a defined period

Art. 32 — Supervisory and enforcement measures for essential entities (proactive, ex-ante supervision):
Competent authorities have powers to:
(a) conduct on-site inspections and off-site supervision, including random checks
(b) request access to data, documents, and information
(c) require security audits carried out by an independent body or competent authority
(d) require the entity to submit to a vulnerability scan
(e) require evidence of implementation of cybersecurity policies
(f) issue binding instructions to remedy identified deficiencies
(g) issue warnings about infringements
(h) order the entity to designate a monitoring officer to oversee compliance for a defined period
(i) require the entity to make aspects of non-compliance with Art. 21 or 23 obligations public

Art. 33 — Supervisory and enforcement measures for important entities (ex-post supervision, triggered by evidence of non-compliance or incident): similar powers but supervision is reactive rather than proactive.

Art. 34 — Administrative fines:
- ESSENTIAL entities: maximum €10 million OR 2% of total worldwide annual turnover in the preceding financial year, whichever is HIGHER
- IMPORTANT entities: maximum €7 million OR 1.4% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is HIGHER

The turnover-based ceiling is structurally similar to GDPR's 4% maximum and is intended to make fines meaningful for large multinationals. Per recital 132, fines should be effective, proportionate and dissuasive, taking into account the duration of the infringement, the financial benefit gained from the infringement, the prior history of the entity, etc.

Member States may additionally impose periodic penalty payments to compel compliance with a binding instruction.

Key practical implications for CISOs:
- Art. 20(1)(c) liability changes how CISOs should structure their reporting and risk-acceptance documentation. Management body sign-offs on residual risk acceptance must be explicit and recorded — verbal approval is no longer enough
- Mandatory management-body training (Art. 20(2)) is increasingly outsourced to law firms and Big-4 advisors offering 'NIS2 board readiness' programmes
- The 2% / 1.4% turnover-based fines have shifted board-level attention from 'is cyber budget reasonable' to 'is non-compliance liability bigger than the budget shortfall' — an important rhetorical pivot for CISO budget conversations
- First major NIS2 enforcement actions are expected H2 2025 / H1 2026 as Member States complete their supervisory build-out

Use this article range when discussing board-level cyber reporting cadence, personal liability for management body members, CISO escalation protocols, the size of the NIS2 fine exposure relative to security investment, or how to structure residual-risk acceptance documentation.
